title: 10 best herbal teas for indigestion
intro: For those seeking natural relief for indigestion, herbal teas can be a great option. Some of the best herbal teas for this purpose include chamomile, ginger, and peppermint. Chamomile is known for its calming properties and can help soothe the stomach. Ginger tea is well-known for its ability to aid in digestion and reduce inflammation, while peppermint tea can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate symptoms of indigestion.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea aids indigestion due to its menthol content, which boasts proven carminative properties. This natural substance found in peppermint leaves boasts the ability to relax gastrointestinal muscles, thereby alleviating symptoms of indigestion. Furthermore, menthol promotes increased bile flow, facilitating faster breakdown and elimination of fats. As a result, peppermint tea consumption brings about reduced gastric inflammation and discomfort. Ultimately, peppermint tea's therapeutic benefits for indigestion stem from its unique organic composition, making it a popular and effective natural remedy.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in volatile oils, the leaves of peppermint plant are widely used to alleviate digestive problems due to their antispasmodic properties.
Flowers: Containing calming compounds, peppermint flowers offer relief from indigestion by reducing inflammation in the digestive tract.
Stems: Packed with essential oils, the stems of peppermint plant promote healthy digestion by aiding in the breakdown of fats and proteins.
tea_recipe: Measure - Boil 1 cup of water and let 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves steep for 5-10 minutes.
Steep - After boiling, remove water from heat and add peppermint leaves, letting them steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain - Strain the tea to remove the peppermint leaves.
Sweeten (optional) - Add honey or lemon to taste.
Serve - Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day after meals.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea aids indigestion due to its carminative properties, which move gas from the digestive tract and relieve bloating. This tea also contains gingerols and shogaols, compounds that accelerate stomach contractions, moving food through the GI tract. Furthermore, ginger tea boasts antiemetic qualities, preventing vomiting and soothing the stomach, thus alleviating indigestion. Additionally, ginger tea's anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, providing additional relief. Lastly, ginger tea enhances the production of digestive enzymes, facilitating the breakdown of food and nutrient absorption, further contributing to indigestion relief.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: Ginger's underground stem, or rhizome, is rich in gingerols and shaogals, which have been shown to help relieve indigestion by speeding up stomach emptying and reducing gastric inflammation.

Roots: The fibrous, spindle-shaped ginger roots contain bioactive compounds that aid in digestion, including gingerols and zingibain, which help relax the intestinal tract and alleviate indigestion symptoms.

Leaves: Ginger leaves offer a milder ginger flavor and contain gingerols and zingibain that promote digestion by increasing saliva and bile production, supporting healthy digestion and reducing indigestion symptoms.

Flowers: Ginger flowers offer a subtle, aromatic ginger flavor and have anti-inflammatory properties, as they contain zingibain and shogaols, which help reduce inflammation and relieve indigestion symptoms, as well as support a healthy digestive system overall.
tea_recipe: Peel and grate 1 inch of ginger, then let it steep in hot water for 5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of honey and 1 tablespoon of lemon juice to the ginger water, stir well.
Warm the mixture if it has cooled, but do not boil.
Strain the tea to remove ginger pieces.
Sip the tea slowly, ideally after a meal, to aid indigestion.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea, known for its soothing properties, brings relief to indigestion by reducing inflammation in the stomach and intestines. This occurs due to its anti-inflammatory compounds, including flavonoids like apigenin and luteolin. Furthermore, chamomile stimulates the production of bile, which is crucial for breaking down fats in the digestive process. As a mild muscle relaxant, it also alleviates gastrointestinal spasms that often lead to discomfort. Additionally, its calming effects on the nervous system can help alleviate stress-related digestive issues.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chamomile's daisy-like flowers are rich in relaxing properties that soothe the digestive system, making them an effective ingredient in teas for indigestion.
Leaves: The delicate leaves of chamomile contain volatile oils which help stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of indigestion.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are a potent source of antispasmodic compounds that aid in relieving gastrointestinal discomfort and easing indigestion.
tea_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for 2-3 minutes until it reaches a temperature of 200°F - 212°F.
Put 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers in a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea to discard the chamomile flowers.
Drink the chamomile tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day after meals.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea offers relief from indigestion due to its carminative properties, which effectively reduce bloating and gas. This herbal remedy stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, accelerating digestion and alleviating discomfort. Moreover, fennel tea's antispasmodic qualities soothe gastrointestinal muscles, averting cramps and spasms. Its volatile oils encourage the expulsion of trapped gas, further easing indigestion. Ultimately, the regular consumption of fennel tea promotes a healthier gastrointestinal tract, minimizing the likelihood of indigestion.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of fennel contain compounds that promote healthy digestion, making them an effective ingredient in medicinal tea for indigestion.
Seeds: Fennel seeds are rich in essential oils that stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of indigestion.
Leaves: The leaves of fennel have antispasmodic properties that soothe the gastrointestinal tract and relieve discomfort from indigestion.
tea_recipe: Chop one tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or two teaspoons of crushed fennel seeds.
Place the chopped fennel seeds in a tea infuser or strainer.
Heat eight ounces of water to boiling, then pour the hot water over the fennel seeds.
Steep the tea for 5-10 minutes, then remove the infuser or strain the tea.
Sip the fennel tea slowly to soothe indigestion.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea aids indigestion due to its active compound, glycyrrhizin, which stimulates the production of mucus in the stomach. This mucus shields the stomach lining from hydrochloric acid, reducing the risk of inflammation and irritation. Moreover, licorice tea enhances digestion by increasing the bile secretion, improving the breakdown of fats and reducing feelings of bloating and discomfort. Additionally, it soothes the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating symptoms of heartburn, acid reflux, and indigestion. Lastly, this herbal tea addresses the root cause of indigestion by fighting harmful bacteria like H. pylori, known to cause stomach ulcers.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe digestive issues.

Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of licorice, a horizontal stem that grows underground, are used in medicinal tea for indigestion due to their demulcent properties, which help protect and soothe the mucous membranes of the digestive tract.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant contain flavonoids that stimulate the production of bile, aiding in the digestion of fats and reducing indigestion symptoms.
Seeds: Licorice seeds contain various compounds, including coumarins and triterpenoids, that have been shown to have anti-spasmodic effects, alleviating digestive discomfort and cramping.

Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant contain essential oils and flavonoids that have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of chopped licorice root with 4 cups of water.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root pieces.
Allow the tea to cool slightly, then drink it in small sips for best results.
Store any remaining tea in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.

teas.tea_desc: Dandelion tea promotes indigestion relief by stimulating the production of bile in the stomach. This bitter herb further aids in breaking down fats, thus speeding up digestion. Moreover, dandelion tea boasts diuretic properties, flushing excess fluids and toxins out of the body, which eases the burden on the digestive system. Finally, its rich fiber content facilitates regular bowel movements, contributing to a balanced gastrointestinal system.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in beneficial compounds, the roots of dandelions promote digestion by stimulating appetite and secretion of digestive juices.
Leaves: High in antioxidants, dandelion leaves help soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, alleviating symptoms of indigestion.
Flowers: Packed with flavonoids, dandelion flowers improve digestion by supporting the production of bile in the liver, aiding in breaking down fats.
tea_recipe: Gather 2-3 dandelion heads with intact yellow flowers and unopened green buds.
Rinse the dandelion heads under cold water to remove dirt and insects.
Coarsely chop the dandelion heads and place them in a teapot or saucepan.
Pour 2 cups of boiling water over the dandelion heads and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solid parts, and enjoy it while warm.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the marshmallow plant's roots, contains a mucilage that soothes and protects the digestive tract when consumed as a hot beverage. This mucilage, a gel-like substance, forms a protective layer on the stomach lining, reducing irritation and inflammation. Moreover, it neutralizes stomach acids and promotes the healing of stomach ulcers. By increasing the mucus production in the gastrointestinal tract, marshmallow tea ensures a smooth digestive process, preventing discomfort and indigestion. Ultimately, the demulcent properties of marshmallow tea make it an effective and reliable remedy to alleviate indigestion symptoms.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in healing properties, the marshmallow root contains mucilage that soothes the digestive tract, providing rapid relief from indigestion.
Leaves: The leaves have long been used for medicinal teas, as they contain anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce gut inflammation, aiding in proper digestion.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers are known for their soothing effects, helping to relax the stomach muscles and alleviate the discomfort caused by indigestion.
Seeds: High in fiber, marshmallow seeds facilitate healthy digestion, easing symptoms of indigestion and supporting overall gastrointestinal health.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water, 1 teaspoon of dried marshmallow root, and 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 15-20 minutes to allow the herbs to release their medicinal properties.
Strain the tea to remove the herbs, then add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Sip the marshmallow tea slowly, allowing it to coat your digestive tract and alleviate indigestion.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea provides immediate relief from indigestion due to its demulcent property, a name given to substances that relieve irritation by coating mucous membranes. This tea, derived from the inner bark of the slippery elm tree, contains mucilage that forms a slick gel when mixed with water, shielding the digestive tract's delicate lining. Consequently, this slimy coating lessens discomfort, inflammation, and irritation in the stomach and intestines, making slippery elm tea an effective natural remedy for indigestion. Furthermore, this soothing drink stimulates digestive juices, promoting proper nutrient absorption and overall gastrointestinal health.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the slippery elm plant contain mucilage, a gel-like substance that soothes digestive inflammation and eases indigestion.
Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm plant is used to make medicinal tea due to its demulcent properties, providing a coating effect in the digestive tract that helps alleviate indigestion.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves have a high mucilage content which aids in digestion by forming a soothing film over the lining of the stomach, helping to reduce irritation and indigestion.
Buds: The buds of the slippery elm plant are rich in antioxidants, making them an excellent addition to a tea for soothing indigestion and promoting overall digestive health.
tea_recipe: Measure - Accurately measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried slippery elm bark.
Steep - Place the measured bark in a cup and pour boiling water over it, allowing it to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain - Strain the tea to remove the bark, leaving behind the liquid.
Sweeten (if desired) - Add a sweetener like honey to taste.
Serve - Pour the tea into a cup and drink it while warm, up to three times a day for indigestion relief.

teas.tea_desc: Skullcap tea relieves indigestion due to its bitterness, which triggers the release of digestive enzymes in the stomach to break down food efficiently. This herbal infusion contains compounds like flavonoids and volatile oils that promote digestion by relaxing gastrointestinal muscles, thus preventing issues such as bloating and gas. Furthermore, skullcap tea's soothing properties help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating discomfort and promoting overall digestive health. Its antispasmodic qualities also ease stomach cramps, further enhancing its effectiveness in treating indigestion.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the skullcap plant are used in medicinal tea for indigestion due to their anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the digestive tract.
Flowers: The flowers of the skullcap plant contain flavonoids that aid in digestion and are often used in medicinal tea to alleviate symptoms of indigestion.
Stems: Skullcap stems contain bitter iridoid glycosides that stimulate digestive juices and are used in medicinal tea to treat indigestion.
Seeds: Skullcap seeds are rich in essential oils and are used in medicinal tea to help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and relieve symptoms of indigestion.
tea_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried skullcap herb to a tea infuser.
Pour the boiling water over the skullcap herb and steep for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser and discard the used herb.
Drink the skullcap tea, up to 3 times a day, to soothe indigestion.

teas.tea_desc: Catnip tea, derived from the Nepeta cataria plant, contains nepetalactone, a compound that relaxes the stomach muscles. This relaxation addresses indigestion by alleviating tension, allowing the digestive system to function smoothly. Moreover, catnip tea has carminative properties, which facilitate the expulsion of gas and decrease bloating. As an added benefit, it reduces anxiety and stress, which often exacerbate indigestion. Finally, its antispasmodic qualities soothe the intestinal tract, further easing discomfort and improving overall digestion.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in calming agents, leaves of catnip plant are effective in alleviating symptoms of indigestion.
Flowers: Packed with therapeutic compounds, catnip flowers promote digestion and soothe the digestive tract.
Stems: High in soothing properties, catnip stems offer relief from discomfort caused by indigestion.
Roots: Bursting with anti-inflammatory properties, catnip roots aid in treating digestive issues and reducing inflammation.
tea_recipe: In a tea infuser, combine 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the beneficial compounds to fully extract.
Remove the tea infuser to prevent over-steeping and bitterness.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste for added flavor.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, for soothing relief of indigestion symptoms.
